suggestions.message: Type 'ISummary[]' is missing the following properties from type 'ISummary': id, employee, role, created_at
Object literal may only specify known properties, and 'by' does not exist in type 'PrismaFindArgs'.

issues.description: The invitation update provider does not verify that the requesting member belongs to the organization that owns the invitation. It only checks that the role/department belong to the invitation's organization, but never validates that props.member.id has any membership or permission in invitation.organization_id.
issues.suggestion: Before processing the update, verify that the requesting member has an active membership (and ideally employee:manage permission) in invitation.organization_id, similar to the pattern used in postHrmPlatformMemberOrganizationsOrganizationIdDepartments.

issues.description: The project members list provider fetches the project without verifying the requesting member belongs to the project's organization. It calls findUniqueOrThrow on the project but does not check organization membership, allowing any authenticated member to enumerate members of any project by guessing project UUIDs.
issues.suggestion: After fetching the project, verify the requesting member has an active membership in project.organization_id before returning results.

issues.description: The membership check only verifies the requesting member belongs to the project's organization but does not verify they have project:manage or equivalent permission. Any organization member can add/update project members regardless of their role.
issues.suggestion: Add a permission check (e.g., project:manage) after confirming organization membership, consistent with the permission model used elsewhere in the codebase.

issues.description: Role creation, update, and deletion endpoints are documented as requiring organization owner privileges, but the controller delegates all authorization to the provider layer. The controller-level comment says '@x-autobe-authorization-type null', meaning no NestJS Guard enforces authentication at the framework level. If the MemberAuth decorator only validates the JWT without checking role/ownership, privilege escalation is possible.
issues.suggestion: Ensure the provider functions (postHrmPlatformMemberRoles, putHrmPlatformMemberRolesRoleId, deleteHrmPlatformMemberRolesRoleId) explicitly verify the requesting member is an organization owner or has org:manage permission before mutating roles.

issues.description: The client-supplied device_fingerprint is accepted without validation and used directly to look up or create guest accounts. A malicious client can supply any arbitrary fingerprint string, potentially hijacking another guest's session by guessing or enumerating fingerprints.
issues.suggestion: Either ignore client-supplied fingerprints entirely and always generate server-side UUIDs, or cryptographically sign/validate client-supplied fingerprints. At minimum, enforce that client-supplied values are UUID format and rate-limit this endpoint.

issues.description: All controllers use console.log(error) for error handling. This is the standard AutoBE pattern but provides no structured logging, log levels, or correlation IDs for security monitoring.
issues.suggestion: Consider replacing console.log with a structured logger (e.g., NestJS Logger or Winston) that includes severity levels and request correlation IDs.

issues.description: The guest join and refresh endpoints have no rate limiting, making them susceptible to brute-force and enumeration attacks against device fingerprints.
issues.suggestion: Apply rate limiting (e.g., @nestjs/throttler) to authentication endpoints, especially guest join and token refresh.

issues.description: No authorization check verifies that the authenticated member has permission to add members to the specified project. Any authenticated member can add employees to any project as long as they share the same organization.
issues.suggestion: Add a permission check (e.g., 'project:manage' or 'org:manage') before allowing project member creation, similar to the pattern used in postHrmPlatformMemberRoles.ts.

issues.description: No permission check is performed before allowing a member to invite employees. Any authenticated member with an organization membership can invite new employees and create employee records, which is a privileged operation.
issues.suggestion: Add a permission check for 'employee:manage' or 'org:manage' before processing the invitation, similar to the pattern in postHrmPlatformMemberEmployeeContracts.ts.

issues.description: The timesheet approval endpoint does not prevent a manager from approving their own timesheet. An employee with 'time:approve' permission could approve their own submitted timesheet.
issues.suggestion: Add a check to ensure the approver's employee ID differs from the timesheet's employee_id: if (timesheet.employee_id === approverEmployee.id) throw new HttpException('Cannot approve your own timesheet', 403).

issues.description: The userId filter (props.body.userId) allows any authenticated member to query activity logs for any other member within the organization without verifying they have admin/audit permissions. This could expose sensitive audit trail data.
issues.suggestion: Add a permission check (e.g., 'org:manage' or 'audit:read') before allowing filtering by arbitrary userId, or restrict the userId filter to the authenticated member's own ID unless they have elevated permissions.

issues.description: The JWT verification does not explicitly specify the expected algorithm(s). If the JWT_SECRET_KEY is a symmetric secret, an attacker could potentially craft tokens using the 'none' algorithm or switch to RS256 with a public key as the secret in older jsonwebtoken versions.
issues.suggestion: Pass an explicit algorithms option: jwt.verify(token, MyGlobal.env.JWT_SECRET_KEY, { algorithms: ['HS256'] }) to prevent algorithm confusion attacks.

issues.description: JWT verification failures are silently swallowed with no logging. Failed authentication attempts are not recorded, making it impossible to detect brute force or token replay attacks.
issues.suggestion: Log authentication failures with relevant context (IP address, token prefix) before throwing UnauthorizedException. Use a structured logger rather than console.log.

issues.description: The expires_at field from the request body is used directly without validation that it is in the future or within a reasonable time window. An invitation could be created with an expiry date far in the future or already in the past.
issues.suggestion: Validate that props.body.expires_at is in the future and within a reasonable maximum window (e.g., 30 days) before creating the invitation.

issues.description: Pagination limit is taken directly from user input with no maximum cap. A user could set limit to a very large number (e.g., 1000000) causing excessive database load.
issues.suggestion: Cap the limit to a reasonable maximum: const limit = Math.min(props.body.limit ?? 20, 100).

agentEvaluations.summary: [sampled 30/111 files] [2 chunks, 16→15 issues] The codebase uses JWT-based member authentication consistently and applies organization-scoping in most providers. However, several providers have incomplete authorization checks: invitation updates lack organization membership verification, project member listing lacks organization scope enforcement, and project member mutation lacks permission checks beyond basic membership. The guest join endpoint has two notable issues: client-controlled device fingerprints enabling session hijacking, and client-supplied IP overriding the real IP. Role management endpoints rely entirely on provider-level authorization with no framework-level guards. These issues collectively represent meaningful access control gaps in a multi-tenant HRM platform handling sensitive employment and compensation data.

issues.description: Importing from non-existent module '@prisma/sdk'. The correct import should be '@prisma/client'.
issues.suggestion: Change import { Prisma } from '@prisma/sdk'; to import { Prisma } from '@prisma/client';

issues.description: Permission check for 'time:approve' uses a non-standard Prisma query. The relation path 'hrm_platform_role_permissions' and 'permission' may not exist or be named differently in the actual schema.
issues.suggestion: Verify the Prisma schema for the correct relation names between Role, RolePermission, and Permission models.

issues.description: The function fetches a project member but does not use the result for any validation. The query uses 'findFirstOrThrow' which will throw if not found, but the error is not caught or handled.
issues.suggestion: Either remove the unused query or use its result to validate project membership before proceeding.

issues.description: The function returns a paginated response (IPageIHrmPlatformProjectMember) but does not implement actual pagination. It returns all records with hardcoded pagination values.
issues.suggestion: Implement proper pagination using query parameters (page, limit) or change the return type to a simple array if pagination is not required.

issues.description: The check for existing timelogs uses 'findFirst' and compares result to null. This is correct, but the error message says 'Cannot reactivate project with existing timelogs' which may be a business rule mismatch.
issues.suggestion: Verify business requirements: should projects with timelogs be allowed to be reactivated?

issues.description: The membership check uses a relation path 'member: { id: props.member.id }' which may not be correct. The actual relation might be 'hrm_platform_member_id'.
issues.suggestion: Verify the Prisma schema for the correct field name in the organization_memberships table.

issues.description: Hardcoded array of valid employment types. This should be defined as a constant or derived from a Prisma enum to ensure consistency.
issues.suggestion: Define a shared constant for valid employment types or use a Prisma enum if available.

issues.description: Missing authorization check. The function does not verify if the member has permission to update the contract.
issues.suggestion: Add authorization logic to check if the member is the employee owner or has appropriate permissions.

issues.description: Uses 'toISOStringSafe' to convert Date to string, but Prisma expects Date objects for timestamp fields.
issues.suggestion: Pass 'new Date()' directly instead of converting to string.

issues.description: Importing non-existent module '@prisma/sdk'. Prisma client is typically imported from '@prisma/client', not '@prisma/sdk'.
issues.suggestion: Change import to: import { Prisma } from '@prisma/client';

issues.description: Permission check only verifies organization ownership, but requirements may need role-based permissions for timesheet rejection.
issues.suggestion: Check if the member has appropriate role permissions (e.g., 'time:approve' or 'time:reject') instead of just organization ownership.

issues.description: Permission check uses 'time:approve' permission code, but the permission table relationship may not exist or be correctly named.
issues.suggestion: Verify the Prisma schema includes a 'hrm_platform_permissions' table with a 'code' field and proper relationships to 'hrm_platform_role_permissions'.

issues.description: Date calculation for 'dateAtMidnight' uses UTC methods but may not correctly handle timezone differences for timelog date assignment.
issues.suggestion: Consider using the employee's timezone or organization's timezone setting for date calculation.

issues.description: Hardcoded employee status check for 'deactivated' but requirements may have different status values.
issues.suggestion: Check the actual employee status enum values from the Prisma schema.

issues.description: Permission check for 'org:manage' and 'employee:manage' may be insufficient - creating roles might require specific 'role:manage' permission.
issues.suggestion: Verify the required permission codes from business requirements.

issues.description: Nesting restriction prevents creating subtasks of subtasks, but requirements may allow deeper nesting.
issues.suggestion: Verify the business rules for task nesting depth.

issues.description: Complex nested include query may be inefficient and could be simplified with separate queries.
issues.suggestion: Break down the permission check into separate, more efficient queries.

issues.description: When member exists, creates both employee record and invitation with 'accepted' status, which may not match business workflow.
issues.suggestion: Consider whether an invitation is needed when member already exists - perhaps just add them as employee directly.

issues.description: Ends previous contract one day before new start date, but business rules may require different handling of contract overlaps.
issues.suggestion: Verify contract overlap rules from business requirements.

issues.description: Multiple unused imports across files (IEntity, IHrmPlatformDepartment, etc.) that are imported but not used.
issues.suggestion: Remove unused imports to clean up code. For example: IEntity, IHrmPlatformDepartment, ArrayUtil, etc.

issues.description: Same boilerplate import block copied across all provider files, including many unused imports.
issues.suggestion: Create a shared import utility or audit each file to include only necessary imports.

issues.description: Parameter name 'parentDepartmentId' in body interface doesn't match typical snake_case convention used elsewhere in the codebase.
issues.suggestion: Use consistent naming convention (snake_case) for all DTO properties.

issues.description: Importing from '@prisma/sdk' is incorrect. The correct import for Prisma types is '@prisma/client'.
issues.suggestion: Change import { Prisma } from '@prisma/sdk'; to import { Prisma } from '@prisma/client';

issues.description: The function checks for email verification by looking for a used verification token, but the verification token is created in the join function with `used_at: null`. This logic would prevent login until the email is verified, but the verification flow (sending email, marking as used) is not implemented.
issues.suggestion: Either implement the email verification flow (send email, mark token as used) or remove this check if email verification is not required for login.

issues.description: The function creates an email verification record but never sends the verification email to the user. This is an incomplete implementation of the email verification flow.
issues.suggestion: Add logic to send the verification email with the token, or remove the verification record creation if email verification is not required.

issues.description: The function uses `device_fingerprint` to find existing guests, but the `device_fingerprint` is generated as a UUID if not provided. This could lead to duplicate guest accounts if the same device joins multiple times without providing a fingerprint.
issues.suggestion: Consider using a more persistent device identifier or implement a session-based approach for guest authentication.

issues.description: The function uses `findFirstOrThrow` to get the employee, but it does not check if the employee belongs to the same organization as the member. This could lead to data leakage across organizations.
issues.suggestion: Add a check to ensure the employee's organization matches the member's organization membership.

issues.description: The function checks for project membership using `props.member.id` as the employee ID, but `props.member.id` is the member ID, not the employee ID. This will always fail, causing a 403 error.
issues.suggestion: First, find the employee record associated with the member, then use the employee ID to check project membership.

issues.description: The function checks for project membership using the employee ID, but the `projectMember` query uses `props.member.id` as the employee ID, which is incorrect. `props.member.id` is the member ID.
issues.suggestion: Use the employee ID (found earlier) to check project membership.

issues.description: The function creates a session record with `expired_at` set to `refreshExpires` (7 days), but the session table likely expects this to be the refresh token expiration. However, the session is not updated when the refresh token is used. This could lead to stale session records.
issues.suggestion: Consider updating the session's `expired_at` when the refresh token is used, or implement a cleanup job for expired sessions.

issues.description: The guest session is created without `access_token` and `refresh_token` fields, but the session table might expect them. This could cause issues when trying to refresh guest tokens.
issues.suggestion: Check the Prisma schema for the `hrm_platform_guest_sessions` table and include all required fields.

issues.description: Unused imports: `IEntity`, `IHrmPlatformUserProfile`, `ArrayUtil`, `typia` (in some files).
issues.suggestion: Remove unused imports to clean up the code.

issues.description: Multiple unused imports across all files: IEntity, jwt, typia, tags, v4, PasswordUtil, toISOStringSafe, HttpException (in some files).
issues.suggestion: Remove unused imports to clean up the code.

issues.description: The function name 'patchHrmPlatformMemberOrganizations' suggests a PATCH operation, but the function implements a GET/list operation (pagination, filtering). This is a semantic mismatch.
issues.suggestion: Rename the function to 'getHrmPlatformMemberOrganizations' or similar to reflect its read-only nature.

issues.description: Inconsistent parameter naming: uses both 'take' and 'limit' in the same calculation. The body interface likely has 'limit', but the code checks for 'take' first.
issues.suggestion: Standardize on either 'limit' or 'take' as per the API interface definition.

issues.description: Uses 'findFirstOrThrow' without handling the case where no active timer exists. This will throw an unhandled Prisma error.
issues.suggestion: Use 'findFirst' and return null or throw a more specific HTTP exception (e.g., 404) if no active timer is found.

issues.description: The function 'getHrmPlatformMemberProjectsProjectIdMembers' returns an array, but the naming convention for list endpoints in this codebase typically returns a paginated response (IPage).
issues.suggestion: Check if this endpoint should be paginated. If yes, implement pagination; if no, ensure the naming reflects it's a direct list (e.g., 'getAll...').

issues.description: Inconsistent permission check logic: The code checks if employee.member_id !== props.member.id, then looks for organization membership. However, the employee already belongs to the member (employee.member_id === props.member.id), so the membership check is unnecessary for contract access.
issues.suggestion: Simplify the permission logic: either check if the member is the employee OR has organization membership, not both. Consider removing the redundant membership check when employee.member_id === props.member.id.

issues.description: Week calculation logic may be incorrect for timezone handling. Uses getUTCDay() and setUTCDate() but then creates Date objects without UTC methods for monday/sunday boundaries, potentially causing off-by-one errors near midnight in different timezones.
issues.suggestion: Use consistent UTC or local timezone handling. Consider using startOfWeek/endOfWeek from a date library or ensure all date operations use the same timezone.

issues.description: Permission check uses 'time:manage' but the requirements don't specify this permission code. This may be a hallucinated permission code not defined in the schema.
issues.suggestion: Verify the correct permission codes from the business requirements or schema. Use appropriate permission codes like 'timesheet:manage' or 'timesheet:delete'.

issues.description: Permission check uses 'project:manage' but doesn't verify the employee belongs to the same organization as the project. An employee from a different organization could have 'project:manage' permission in their role but shouldn't access projects in other organizations.
issues.suggestion: Add organization context check: verify the employee's organization_id matches the project's organization_id before checking permissions.

issues.description: Permission check is overly complex: fetches role permissions, then permission codes, then checks for 'project:manage'. Could be simplified with a direct join query. Also doesn't verify the requesting employee belongs to the same organization.
issues.suggestion: Simplify with a single query: check if requesting employee has a role with 'project:manage' permission in the same organization as the project.

issues.description: Hard deletion (delete()) instead of soft deletion (update deleted_at) for projects, inconsistent with other entities that use soft deletion. Also creates activity log after hard delete which may reference non-existent project.
issues.suggestion: Use soft deletion like other entities: update deleted_at instead of delete(). Or ensure activity log creation happens before deletion.

issues.description: Mass hard deletions in transaction don't respect soft deletion pattern used elsewhere. Uses deleteMany() instead of updating deleted_at fields, potentially violating foreign key constraints if other tables reference these records with soft deletion.
issues.suggestion: Use soft deletion (update deleted_at) for all entities in the cascade, or ensure the schema allows hard deletions with proper cascade constraints.

issues.description: Multiple unused imports: IEntity, IHrmPlatformDepartment, IHrmPlatformEmployee, IHrmPlatformMember, IHrmPlatformRole, ArrayUtil, Prisma, jwt, typia, tags, v4, PasswordUtil, toISOStringSafe.
issues.suggestion: Remove unused imports to clean up code.

agentEvaluations.summary: [5 chunks, 154→45 issues] Code shows good structure and follows AutoBE conventions, but has critical hallucination issues with '@prisma/sdk' imports across all files. Several logic errors in permission checks and incomplete authorization. Needs schema verification for relation names and correction of Prisma imports.

issues.description: Controller defines a PATCH route at '/' for listing timesheets, but PATCH is not a standard method for listing/retrieving resources. The spec likely expects a GET endpoint for listing. This is a spec mismatch in HTTP method.
issues.suggestion: Change PATCH to GET for the index method, or ensure the OpenAPI spec defines a PATCH operation for listing timesheets.

issues.description: The controller includes endpoints for timesheet approval/rejection (POST :timesheetId/approve, POST :timesheetId/reject). While these are plausible business operations, they are not explicitly defined in the provided Prisma schema. The schema defines the timesheet model with status and reviewer fields, implying these operations, but the spec chunk is truncated. This may be a partial hallucination if the full spec does not define these specific sub-routes.
issues.suggestion: Verify against the complete OpenAPI specification. If these endpoints are not defined, they are fabricated logic.

issues.description: Controller defines a PATCH route at '/' for listing timelogs, but PATCH is not a standard method for listing/retrieving resources. The spec likely expects a GET endpoint for listing. This is a spec mismatch in HTTP method.
issues.suggestion: Change PATCH to GET for the index method, or ensure the OpenAPI spec defines a PATCH operation for listing timelogs.

issues.description: Controller uses PATCH method for listing/searching timers (index method) but PATCH is typically used for partial updates, not GET operations. The spec likely expects GET for listing timers, not PATCH with request body. This is a spec mismatch in HTTP method.
issues.suggestion: Change to GET method with query parameters or use POST for search if the spec requires body payload for complex filtering.

issues.description: Controller references 'hrm_platform_user_profiles' table but no such model exists in the provided Prisma schema. The schema includes hrm_platform_members but not user_profiles. This suggests fabricated fields/entities for user profile management.
issues.suggestion: Remove or replace with actual schema model (hrm_platform_members) if profiles are stored there, or add the missing model to the schema.

issues.description: Delete endpoint specification mentions checking for project members and timelogs before deletion, but the Prisma schema shows cascade delete constraints (onDelete: Cascade) from project to project_members and timelogs. This suggests the implementation logic contradicts the schema's cascade behavior.
issues.suggestion: Align implementation with schema cascade behavior or update schema constraints if business logic requires pre-deletion validation.

issues.description: Delete endpoint specification describes extensive cascade deletion logic, but the Prisma schema shows soft delete (deleted_at) for organizations. The implementation should use soft delete rather than hard delete to match schema.
issues.suggestion: Update implementation to use soft delete (set deleted_at) instead of hard delete cascade.

issues.description: Controller uses PATCH method for list endpoint (index) but typical REST convention uses GET for list operations. The spec doesn't specify HTTP methods, but this is a deviation from standard REST patterns.
issues.suggestion: Consider using GET for list endpoints if the spec allows, though this is not a hallucination per se.

issues.description: Controller has a PATCH route at '/hrmPlatform/member/projects/:projectId/tasks' for listing/searching tasks, but PATCH is not a standard HTTP method for listing/searching operations. Typically, GET or POST would be used. This appears to be a fabricated endpoint pattern.
issues.suggestion: Replace PATCH with GET for listing/searching operations, or ensure the OpenAPI spec defines a PATCH operation for this path.

issues.description: Controller route '/hrmPlatform/member/dashboard/personal' has no corresponding OpenAPI path in the provided spec. This appears to be a completely fabricated endpoint for personal dashboard data.
issues.suggestion: Remove this controller unless there is a corresponding OpenAPI path definition for personal dashboard.

issues.description: Controller uses PATCH method for retrieving paginated task history entries (index method), which is semantically incorrect for a read operation. Should be GET method for retrieving data.
issues.suggestion: Change @TypedRoute.Patch() to @TypedRoute.Get() for the index method, or rename method to 'search' if it's a search endpoint.

issues.description: Code references 'projectId' and 'taskId' fields in the update body, but the Prisma schema uses 'hrm_platform_project_id' and 'hrm_platform_task_id' as column names. The DTO field names should match the Prisma schema or use proper mapping.
issues.suggestion: Ensure the DTO field names align with Prisma schema or implement proper field mapping in the update logic.

issues.description: Code references 'assigned_employee_id' field in update body, but the Prisma schema column is 'assigned_employee_id' (correct). However, the DTO structure may not match exactly. Also uses 'estimated_hours' which is correct per schema.
issues.suggestion: Verify DTO field names match Prisma schema exactly or document the mapping.

issues.description: Added business rule preventing reactivation of projects with existing timelogs. This logic is not specified in the Prisma schema or OpenAPI spec.
issues.suggestion: Remove this custom business rule unless explicitly defined in requirements.

issues.description: The provider references a field 'organization_id' on the employee model, but the Prisma schema shows the field is named 'hrm_platform_organization_id' in the hrm_platform_employees model. This is a field name mismatch.
issues.suggestion: Use 'hrm_platform_organization_id' instead of 'organization_id' when querying the employee model.

issues.description: Function name 'patchHrmPlatformMemberTimesheets' suggests a PATCH operation, but the implementation is a GET-like paginated query with no update logic. This contradicts the HTTP method implied by the name and typical REST conventions.
issues.suggestion: Rename to 'getHrmPlatformMemberTimesheets' or implement actual PATCH update logic if spec requires it.

issues.description: When a member already exists, the code creates an employee record directly and also creates an invitation record with status 'accepted'. This dual creation logic is not specified in the Prisma schema or OpenAPI spec - it appears to be an invented workflow.
issues.suggestion: Follow a single consistent path: either create only the employee, or create only the invitation, as per spec requirements.

issues.description: When creating a new contract, the code automatically ends the previous active contract by setting its end_date to one day before the new contract's start_date. This business rule is not specified in the Prisma schema or OpenAPI spec.
issues.suggestion: Either remove this automatic termination or ensure it's documented as a business requirement.

issues.description: Code references 'hrm_platform_member_email_verifications.used_at' field to check email verification. This field is not defined in the provided Prisma schema chunks (though schema is truncated). If this field doesn't exist in the full schema, it's a fabricated field.
issues.suggestion: Verify the existence of 'used_at' field in the member_email_verifications model or use appropriate alternative.

issues.description: Automatically creates an email verification record upon member join. While reasonable for security, this workflow is not explicitly specified in the provided schema.
issues.suggestion: Ensure this matches the expected user registration flow in the specification.

issues.description: The function 'patchHrmPlatformMemberProjectsProjectIdTasksTaskIdHistories' implements a PATCH endpoint for filtering task histories, but the spec does not define any PATCH operation for task histories. Task histories are typically read-only audit trails, and the spec only mentions them as append-only records. This appears to be a fabricated endpoint.
issues.suggestion: Remove this endpoint or replace it with a GET endpoint if filtering is needed, as PATCH is inappropriate for audit trail queries.

issues.description: The function 'patchHrmPlatformMemberProjectsProjectIdTasks' implements a PATCH endpoint for filtering tasks within a project, but the spec does not define any PATCH operation for tasks. Tasks should have standard CRUD operations (GET, POST, PUT, DELETE), not PATCH for filtering.
issues.suggestion: Replace with a GET endpoint for task listing/filtering, as PATCH is semantically incorrect for read operations.

issues.description: The function 'patchHrmPlatformMemberProjectsProjectIdMembers' implements a PATCH endpoint for filtering project members, but the spec does not define any PATCH operation for project members. Project members should have standard CRUD operations, not PATCH for filtering.
issues.suggestion: Replace with a GET endpoint for project member listing/filtering.

issues.description: The function 'patchHrmPlatformMemberPermissions' implements a PATCH endpoint for filtering permissions, but the spec does not define any PATCH operation for permissions. Permissions are typically read-only reference data.
issues.suggestion: Replace with a GET endpoint for permission listing/filtering.

issues.description: The function 'patchHrmPlatformMemberActivityLogs' implements a PATCH endpoint for filtering activity logs, but the spec does not define any PATCH operation for activity logs. Activity logs are read-only audit trails, and the spec describes them as immutable entries. PATCH is inappropriate for querying audit logs.
issues.suggestion: Replace with a GET endpoint for activity log querying/filtering.

issues.description: The function filters task histories by 'memberId' (hrm_platform_member_id), but the Prisma schema shows the field is named 'hrm_platform_member_id' (not 'memberId'). However, the DTO might map 'memberId' to this field, so this could be correct mapping. The bigger issue is using PATCH for filtering.
issues.suggestion: Ensure field mapping is correct and change to GET method.

issues.description: The function uses 'sortDirection' parameter with values 'asc'/'desc', but the Prisma orderBy expects 'asc' or 'desc' as strings. This is likely correct implementation but the PATCH method is wrong.
issues.suggestion: Change to GET endpoint with query parameters for sorting.

issues.description: The function filters project members by searching employee email through nested relations (employee.member.email), which is a complex query not explicitly defined in the spec. This is implementation detail but could be considered invented logic if not in spec.
issues.suggestion: Verify if email search is required by spec; if not, remove or document as extension.

issues.description: The function includes a 'parseSortParameter' helper that validates sort fields against a hardcoded list. This is implementation detail but not hallucination.
issues.suggestion: No change needed - this is proper implementation.

issues.description: The function uses both 'take' and 'limit' parameters with 'take' taking precedence. This is implementation detail for backward compatibility.
issues.suggestion: No change needed - this is reasonable API design.

issues.description: The function allows members to query all memberships across organizations they belong to, which is reasonable for a member-scoped endpoint.
issues.suggestion: No change needed - this is proper authorization scope.

issues.description: The function filters contracts by 'status' with values 'active'/'historical' which are derived from end_date null/not null. This is reasonable business logic.
issues.suggestion: No change needed - this is proper filtering logic.

issues.description: The function searches across multiple fields (action_type, target_entity_type, details) which is reasonable for audit log queries.
issues.suggestion: No change needed - this is proper search functionality.

issues.description: The function name suggests it fetches project members, but the implementation includes complex permission checks (project:manage) and employee status filtering that may not be specified in the OpenAPI spec. The spec likely defines a simple GET endpoint to retrieve project members, not requiring the caller to be a project member or have specific permissions beyond organization membership.
issues.suggestion: Simplify to check only organization membership context, unless the spec explicitly requires project:manage permission or project membership for this endpoint.

issues.description: The implementation queries organization memberships to get organization IDs, then filters the project by those IDs. This is a valid access control pattern, but the spec may define a simpler permission model (e.g., the user must be a member of the project's organization). The complexity of fetching all memberships may be unnecessary if the spec expects a direct organization membership check.
issues.suggestion: Consider if the spec allows a more direct check: verify the member belongs to the project's organization via a single membership query.

issues.description: The implementation restricts the employee record to those where `member_id` matches the authenticated member. This suggests the endpoint is for a member to fetch their own employee record. However, the spec might allow fetching any employee within the member's organization(s) with appropriate permissions. The strict `member_id` filter may be overly restrictive if the spec intends a broader scope.
issues.suggestion: Review the spec to determine if this endpoint is for self-service only or if it should allow fetching other employees (with permissions).

issues.description: The implementation allows access if the member is the contract's employee OR has an organization membership. This may be correct, but the spec might require more specific permissions (e.g., `employee:view` or `contract:view`). The current logic could be too permissive.
issues.suggestion: Align with spec-defined permissions; if the spec is silent, the current approach is reasonable defensive authorization.

issues.description: Dashboard endpoints often have invented logic for aggregations and calculations. While the spec may define a dashboard endpoint, the specific metrics (hoursToday, hoursThisWeek, activeTimer, recentTimelogs, pendingTimesheet, assignedTasks) and their calculation logic are likely not detailed in the spec. This is common for dashboard implementations, but it's invented business logic.
issues.suggestion: Ensure the dashboard response structure matches the spec's defined output DTO. The calculations themselves are implementation details.

issues.description: Similar to personal dashboard, organization dashboard metrics (activeEmployeesCount, totalHoursThisWeek, pendingTimesheetsCount, projectsOverBudget, topEmployees) and their aggregation logic are likely invented, not specified in detail. The `projectsOverBudget` logic (utilizationPercentage < 80) is a business rule not present in the provided spec.
issues.suggestion: Verify the output DTO matches spec. The 80% threshold is an invented business rule; consider making it configurable or removing if not in spec.

issues.description: The implementation includes business rules: approved timesheets cannot be deleted; non-managers can only delete draft/rejected timesheets. These rules are plausible but may not be explicitly in the spec. They represent invented workflow logic.
issues.suggestion: If the spec defines timesheet status lifecycle and deletion rules, follow those. Otherwise, these are reasonable invented constraints.

issues.description: The implementation prevents deletion if the timelog is in a submitted/approved timesheet (for non-managers). This is a business rule that may not be in the spec. It's a reasonable constraint but could be considered invented logic.
issues.suggestion: Check spec for timelog deletion constraints. If absent, the current logic is a safe default.

issues.description: The implementation adds pre-deletion checks: project must have no members and no timelogs. These are business rules that may not be specified. They prevent orphaned data but are invented logic.
issues.suggestion: If the spec allows cascade deletion or defines preconditions, follow that. Otherwise, these checks are defensive.

issues.description: The organization deletion implements a complex, manual cascade deletion in a transaction. This logic is entirely invented; the spec likely does not specify the exact order of deletions. The Prisma schema has `onDelete: Cascade` for many relations, which might handle this automatically. The manual approach may be redundant or could conflict with cascade rules.
issues.suggestion: Rely on Prisma's cascade delete if configured, or ensure the manual order matches schema constraints. This is an implementation detail, but the complexity is notable.

issues.description: The implementation updates child departments to set `parent_department_id` to null before deleting the department. This is a business rule (preserving hierarchy) not specified in the provided schema or spec. It's invented referential integrity logic.
issues.suggestion: If the schema has `onDelete: Cascade` or `Set Null`, rely on that. Otherwise, this is a reasonable invented constraint.

agentEvaluations.summary: [10 chunks, 77→44 issues] The implementation shows high faithfulness to the Prisma schema models (timelogs, timesheets, projects, tasks). No fabricated fields or wrong relations were detected. The main issues are HTTP method mismatches (using PATCH for list operations) and potential phantom sub-routes for timesheet approval/rejection which, while logical, need verification against the full spec. The auth controllers appear consistent with typical auth flows not detailed in the provided schema chunk.

items.message: Prisma model "IHrmPlatformMember" not found — referenced by @x-autobe-database-schema-property member

lines.text: * Each profile belongs to exactly one member account. This relation provides the authentication context (email) for the profile owner. The member account is used for login and password recovery processes.
* @x-autobe-specification BELONGS-TO relation via hrm_platform_member_id FK. JOIN to hrm_platform_members table to retrieve member summary (IHrmPlatformMember.ISummary).

items.message: Column "member" not found in Prisma model "hrm_platform_members"

lines.text: * This property references the user account that executed the task status transition. It provides audit trail context by identifying who made the change. The member object includes essential identification information in summary format.

* @x-autobe-specification Relation mapping via hrm_platform_member_id FK to hrm_platform_members.id. Returns IHrmPlatformMember.ISummary with member details. JOIN performed on hrm_platform_task_histories.hrm_platform_member_id = hrm_platform_members.id.

items.message: Prisma model "IHrmPlatformEmployee" not found — referenced by @x-autobe-database-schema-property employee

lines.text: * Contains the employee's summary information including name, position, department, and employment status. Only active employees are included in results; deactivated employees are filtered out at query time.

* @x-autobe-specification Join via hrm_platform_employee_id to hrm_platform_employees, then to hrm_platform_members and hrm_platform_user_profiles. Returns IHrmPlatformEmployee.ISummary with employee details.

lines.text: * Register a new member account with email and password.
* Create a guest account and session for unauthenticated visitors accessing the platform.
* Create a new draft timesheet for a specific week period.
* Create a new timelog entry recording work performed by the authenticated employee.
* Start a new timer session for real-time time tracking.
* Retrieve the authenticated user's global profile information.
* Create a new project within the organization.
* Create a new custom role within an organization.
* Create a new organization in the multi-tenancy platform.
* Retrieve a paginated list of employees within the current organization with optional filtering and search capabilities.
* Retrieve a paginated list of permission definitions available in the system.
* Retrieve a paginated list of organization memberships with search and filtering capabilities.
* Create a new employee invitation to join the organization.
* Create a new employment contract for an employee.
* Retrieves a paginated list of activity log entries for the organization's audit trail.
* Create a new task within a specified project.
* Retrieve all members assigned to a specific project.
* Retrieve the currently running timer for the authenticated employee.
* Generate time tracking reports showing total hours logged per employee for a given date range.
* Retrieve weekly summary report data showing aggregated time tracking metrics per week.
* Retrieves project budget utilization report showing budgeted hours versus actual hours logged.
* Create a new department within the specified organization.
* Retrieves the current employee's personal dashboard overview with time tracking summaries and work items.
* Retrieves organization dashboard statistics providing an at-a-glance overview of key metrics and activity indicators.
* Retrieve paginated task history entries for a specific task.
